coaching.subject: the exact help-text substring to assert

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete, unique substring that the updated `--help` text must contain and that would not appear before this change (e.g. `'code-review'` gate label plus a `project.` example like `project.security`), so the AC is unambiguously falsifiable.
evidence: 'e.g. `project.<name>` under the code-review gate' — the 'e.g.' makes the required substring illustrative rather than normative
'asserting the substring is present in the parser's `--help` text' — no specific substring is mandated
findings.finding: AC3's help-text assertion leaves the exact substring to assert unspecified ('e.g. project.<name> under the code-review gate'), so the executor must choose what to assert; a trivially weak assertion (e.g. asserting 'project.' which already appears in plan-review examples) could satisfy the AC without proving the code-review gate example was actually added.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 / Testing > Help text bullet
scenarios: Executor picks a substring that pre-existed the change, making the test green without the help-text update
Different executors choose different substrings, producing inconsistent test coverage
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'e.g. `project.<name>` under the code-review gate' with a pinned example such as `project.security` and require the test to assert `'project.security'` (or whatever name the synthetic-repo fixture activates) appears in `--help` output.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (in the Approach section) that a plan-review id colliding with a code-review id transitions from 'admitted' to 'ambiguous-rejected', and either document a rollback/escape path (e.g. note that the rejected `--gate` flag is the remediation if collision occurs) or confirm such collisions are structurally impossible (e.g. namespaced prefixes enforce uniqueness).
evidence: Current preflight only consults plan-review registry, so a plan-review id always passes admission today.
Post-change: dual-registry lookup means a plan-review id that also appears in code-review registry is rejected as ambiguous — a new rejection for a previously-admitted id.
No rollback path or expand-contract sequence is described for this scenario.
findings.finding: A plan-review id that also exists in the code-review registry currently gets admitted (plan-review lookup succeeds); after this change it would be rejected as ambiguous. This is an unacknowledged behavioral regression for that cross-gate case, with no rollback or expand-contract path described.
findings.location: Approach section — 'If the id is active in both gates, reject as ambiguous'
scenarios: A team has a plan-review criterion whose string id happens to match a newly-registered code-review criterion; previously `criteria eval <id>` worked; after the change it fails as ambiguous with no workaround short of renaming one of the criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence note in Approach: 'Because plan-review and code-review criterion ids use distinct namespacing (e.g. `project.<name>` is code-review-only), genuine cross-registry collisions are structurally impossible; if a collision is detected the ambiguous-rejection message instructs the operator to rename one criterion.' If namespacing does NOT guarantee uniqueness, reinstate the `--gate` flag as an explicit override escape hatch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `CriteriaError` (or its `RegistryError` alias) to the import and catch clause in the new dual-registry preflight block in `_llm_eval_commands.py`, matching the existing `LLMError` pattern.
evidence: `effective_criteria` in `src/rebar/llm/criteria/overlay.py:276` raises `CriteriaError` (not `LLMError`) on a malformed overlay.
`plan_review.registry.by_id` delegates to `load_criteria`, which re-raises prompt-load failures as `RegistryError` (an alias for `CriteriaError` at `registry.py:58`), not `LLMError`.

The existing preflight block (lines 144–155) only catches `LLMError`; the plan's scope section lists only one edit target for exception handling and does not mention adding a `CriteriaError` / `RegistryError` catch or import.

ABSENCE: The plan's Scope section says only '`src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` — dual-registry admission, one-gate resolution, ambiguity rejection, help text' with no mention of a `CriteriaError` import or catch clause.
findings.finding: `CriteriaError` is an unenumerated exception type that the new preflight code path can raise, but the plan's scope section does not list it as a missing import/catch in `_llm_eval_commands.py`.
scenarios: A user with a malformed `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` runs `rebar criteria eval project.foo`; the new `effective_criteria(repo_root)` call raises `CriteriaError`, which propagates uncaught past the `except LLMError` block, producing an unhandled exception traceback instead of a clean error message.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `_llm_eval_commands.py`, add: 'import and catch `CriteriaError` (from `rebar.llm.criteria.model`) alongside `LLMError` in the expanded preflight block, so a malformed overlay yields a clean `Error:` message rather than an uncaught exception.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for the dual-registry-in-preflight approach (e.g., 'the CLI preflight is the correct seam for gate resolution because it keeps `calibrate_criterion` gate-agnostic at the call boundary and is consistent with the existing preflight-then-dispatch structure in `_criteria_eval`').
evidence: Rejected alternative paragraph only explains why --gate was rejected, never affirmatively states why the dual-lookup approach is a good fit
No positive rationale for: why doing the gate resolution in the CLI preflight (rather than inside calibrate_criterion) is architecturally correct
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for WHY the dual-registry-in-preflight approach is the right fit — it only states what is rejected (the --gate flag), not why this design aligns with the system's patterns (e.g., keeping calibrate_criterion gate-agnostic at the call boundary, consistent with the existing preflight-then-dispatch structure).
scenarios: An implementer uncertain whether gate resolution belongs in the CLI or in calibrate_criterion has no design principle to anchor the decision
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add one sentence such as: 'The CLI preflight is the correct seam for gate resolution because it keeps `calibrate_criterion` gate-agnostic at its call boundary and mirrors the existing preflight-then-dispatch structure already in place at lines 143–158.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
